policy_statement: Provide consumer and business-to-business education on bio-based and low-carbon products to build buyer confidence.
mechanism_description: NYSERDA should provide consumer and business-to-business education on bio-based and low-carbon products to build buyer confidence and support greater uptake of lower-carbon products.
primary_causal_pathway: Information/education → product choice shift (low-carbon substitution) → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Consumer and B2B procurement behavior shifts toward bio-based/low-carbon products due to improved awareness, trust, and perceived performance. (2) Mediating process: Market substitution and scale-up—lower-carbon materials/products displace higher-carbon alternatives, often lowering upstream manufacturing emissions and/or embodied carbon across supply chains. (3) Outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions (scope varies by product category), contributing to mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing lifecycle GHG emissions by increasing adoption of lower-carbon products that displace higher-carbon incumbents.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: education → increased adoption of low-carbon products → reduced lifecycle emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while education can also improve resource efficiency indirectly, the stated intent and primary physical outcome is lower GHG intensity through product substitution, which is mitigation rather than efficiency as the core objective.  
3) Edge cases applied: if “bio-based” products are not actually lower-carbon (e.g., land-use change emissions, short-lived carbon storage), realized mitigation could be ambiguous; however, the canonical mechanism is explicitly “low-carbon product adoption,” so the dominant intended effect remains emissions reduction.  
4) Applies to all instances: any policy that educates buyers specifically to increase uptake of low-carbon products operates through demand-side substitution to reduce lifecycle emissions, fitting Mitigation consistently.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., industrial decarbonization, embodied carbon, procurement/consumption behavior) is deferred to policy-level evidence and the specific product categories targeted.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity in actual net GHG benefits for some “bio-based” products depending on feedstocks, land-use change, and carbon accounting boundaries; education alone may have uncertain effectiveness, but the dominant intended pathway remains mitigation through low-carbon substitution.
key_indicators: “provide consumer and business-to-business education”; “bio-based products and low-carbon products”; “build buyer confidence”
co_benefits: Potential market development/innovation support for bio-based industries; improved product literacy and reduced perceived performance risk for purchasers.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although framed as “education” (not necessarily disclosure of quantified emissions), it still fits best under information-disclosure as a public information/awareness instrument rather than regulation or incentives.
